# Dependencies in ASP.NET Core Interview Questions

![Dependencies in ASP.NET Core Interview Questions](../../../assets/dependency-injection-flow.svg)

This guide covers practical dependency injection and service-composition design in ASP.NET Core. It follows the corrected format of **100 interview questions for each subtopic**, and every answer includes a C# code example with rotated real-world scenarios so the examples do not repeat verbatim.

## How To Use This Page

- Questions 1-100 cover Dependency injection container.
- Questions 101-200 cover Service registration.
- Questions 201-300 cover Service lifetimes.
- Questions 301-400 cover Constructor injection.
- Questions 401-500 cover Interface-based design.
- Questions 501-600 cover Options pattern.
- Questions 601-700 cover Scoped services in web requests.
- Questions 701-800 cover Testing and mocking.
- Questions 801-900 cover Anti-patterns.
- Questions 901-1000 cover Troubleshooting resolution issues.

## 1. Dependency injection container

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Dependency injection container**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q1.1 What is built in DI container role in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Built in di container role means ASP.NET Core includes a built-in container that creates services and resolves dependency graphs at runtime. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { }
        class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}
```

### Q1.2 How does composition root concept in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Composition root concept means service wiring belongs in the startup composition root where application dependencies are declared intentionally. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with registration scattered through feature code, and they should avoid the trap of losing control of how the graph is assembled. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<IClock, SystemClock>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
    }
}
```

### Q1.3 Why does container versus object creation by hand in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container versus object creation by hand means the container helps centralize creation rules lifetimes and replacement seams. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual creation in every layer, and they should avoid the trap of duplicating construction logic across the app. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new InvoiceService(new SystemClock());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}
```

### Q1.4 When should you use container limits and responsibility in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container limits and responsibility means the built-in container is meant for straightforward composition rather than arbitrary runtime service location. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with container as a magic toolbox, and they should avoid the trap of using it as a replacement for design discipline. Example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool containerIsMagic = false;
        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}
```

### Q1.5 What problem does dependency graph resolution flow in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Dependency graph resolution flow means service resolution follows constructor dependencies recursively based on registrations and lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with resolution as unexplained magic, and they should avoid the trap of debugging graphs without understanding the dependency chain. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a controller refactor, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class Handler { public Handler(IClock clock) { } }
        interface IClock { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(Handler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q1.6 How would you explain container interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container interview framing means strong answers connect the container to startup composition, lifetimes, and maintainability. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with terminology only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime behavior. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("The composition root controls how services are wired");
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. ### Q1.32 How can composition root concept in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Composition root concept means service wiring belongs in the startup composition root where application dependencies are declared intentionally. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with registration scattered through feature code, and they should avoid the trap of losing control of how the graph is assembled. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;

using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{
    public static void Run()
    {

        builder.Services.AddSingleton<IClock, SystemClock>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
    }
}
```

### Q1.33 What is container versus object creation by hand in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container versus object creation by hand means the container helps centralize creation rules lifetimes and replacement seams. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual creation in every layer, and they should avoid the trap of duplicating construction logic across the app. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_33
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new InvoiceService(new SystemClock());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}
```

### Q1.34 How does container limits and responsibility in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container limits and responsibility means the built-in container is meant for straightforward composition rather than arbitrary runtime service location. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with container as a magic toolbox, and they should avoid the trap of using it as a replacement for design discipline. Example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_34
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool containerIsMagic = false;
        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}
```

### Q1.35 Why does dependency graph resolution flow in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Dependency graph resolution flow means service resolution follows constructor dependencies recursively based on registrations and lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with resolution as unexplained magic, and they should avoid the trap of debugging graphs without understanding the dependency chain. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_35
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class Handler { public Handler(IClock clock) { } }
        interface IClock { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(Handler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with terminology only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime behavior. Example: during a service-registration review, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_60
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("The composition root controls how services are wired");
    }
}
```

### Q1.61 What problem does built in DI container role in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Built in di container role means ASP.NET Core includes a built-in container that creates services and resolves dependency graphs at runtime. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_61
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { }
        class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}
```

### Q1.62 How would you explain composition root concept in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Composition root concept means service wiring belongs in the startup composition root where application dependencies are declared intentionally. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with registration scattered through feature code, and they should avoid the trap of losing control of how the graph is assembled. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_62
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<IClock, SystemClock>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
    }
}
```

### Q1.63 Why is container versus object creation by hand in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container versus object creation by hand means the container helps centralize creation rules lifetimes and replacement seams. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual creation in every layer, and they should avoid the trap of duplicating construction logic across the app. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_63
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new InvoiceService(new SystemClock());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}
```

### Q1.64 How can container limits and responsibility in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container limits and responsibility means the built-in container is meant for straightforward composition rather than arbitrary runtime service location. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with container as a magic toolbox, and they should avoid the trap of using it as a replacement for design discipline. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_64
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool containerIsMagic = false;
        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}
```

### Q1.65 What is dependency graph resolution flow in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Dependency graph resolution flow means service resolution follows constructor dependencies recursively based on registrations and lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with resolution as unexplained magic, and they should avoid the trap of debugging graphs without understanding the dependency chain. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_65
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class Handler { public Handler(IClock clock) { } }
        interface IClock { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(Handler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with terminology only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime behavior. Example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower.

```csharp
}
```

### Q1.91 Why does built in DI container role in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Built in di container role means ASP.NET Core includes a built-in container that creates services and resolves dependency graphs at runtime. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual new chains everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring the container as the application composition mechanism. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_91
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { }
        class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}
```

### Q1.92 When should you use composition root concept in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Composition root concept means service wiring belongs in the startup composition root where application dependencies are declared intentionally. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with registration scattered through feature code, and they should avoid the trap of losing control of how the graph is assembled. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_92
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<IClock, SystemClock>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
    }
}
```

### Q1.93 What problem does container versus object creation by hand in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container versus object creation by hand means the container helps centralize creation rules lifetimes and replacement seams. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with manual creation in every layer, and they should avoid the trap of duplicating construction logic across the app. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo1_93
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new InvoiceService(new SystemClock());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock) { } }
    }
}
```

### Q1.94 How would you explain container limits and responsibility in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container limits and responsibility means the built-in container is meant for straightforward composition rather than arbitrary runtime service location. Teams should focus on it when explaining dependency injection container in real systems, they compare it with container as a magic toolbox, and they should avoid the trap of using it as a replacement for design discipline. Example: during a controller refactor, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

```csharp
using System.Linq;

using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{

    {

        Console.WriteLine(containerIsMagic);
    }
}
```

**Answer:** Dependency graph resolution flow means service resolution follows constructor dependencies recursively based on registrations and lifetimes. ## 2. Service registration

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Service registration**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q2.1 What problem does registration by contract and implementation in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Registration by contract and implementation means services are commonly registered against interfaces or concrete types to control how implementations are resolved. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with concrete new statements everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of registering services without design intent. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IEmailSender { }
        class SmtpEmailSender : IEmailSender { }
    }
}
```

### Q2.2 How would you explain registration style conventions in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Registration style conventions means consistent registration conventions improve readability and onboarding in larger apps. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with each file invents its own pattern, and they should avoid the trap of letting service setup become inconsistent noise. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        string convention = "AddFeatureServices";
        Console.WriteLine(convention);
    }
}
```

### Q2.3 Why is extension methods for service groups in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Extension methods for service groups means teams often group related registrations behind extension methods to keep Program.cs readable. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with one huge startup block, and they should avoid the trap of hiding registration logic without meaningful grouping. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IReportService, ReportService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IExportService, ExportService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IReportService { } interface IExportService { } class ReportService : IReportService { } class ExportService : IExportService { }
    }
}
```

### Q2.4 How can registration order awareness in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Registration order awareness means some registrations override or decorate earlier ones so order can matter in selected scenarios. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with order never matters, and they should avoid the trap of changing order blindly. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool orderSensitive = true;
        Console.WriteLine(orderSensitive);
    }
}
```

### Q2.5 What is feature-based service modules in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Feature-based service modules means feature or layer-based registration can make service setup more maintainable as the app grows. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with all registrations in one undifferentiated block, and they should avoid the trap of letting composition root sprawl uncontrollably. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

```csharp
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

    {
        string module = "OrdersModule";
        Console.WriteLine(module);
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with all registrations in one undifferentiated block, and they should avoid the trap of letting composition root sprawl uncontrollably. ### Q2.55 Why does registration by contract and implementation in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Registration by contract and implementation means services are commonly registered against interfaces or concrete types to control how implementations are resolved. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with concrete new statements everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of registering services without design intent. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_55
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IEmailSender { }
        class SmtpEmailSender : IEmailSender { }
    }
}
```

### Q2.57 What problem does extension methods for service groups in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Extension methods for service groups means teams often group related registrations behind extension methods to keep Program.cs readable. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with one huge startup block, and they should avoid the trap of hiding registration logic without meaningful grouping. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo2_57
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IReportService, ReportService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IExportService, ExportService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IReportService { } interface IExportService { } class ReportService : IReportService { } class ExportService : IExportService { }
    }
}
```

### Q2.58 How would you explain registration order awareness in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Registration order awareness means some registrations override or decorate earlier ones so order can matter in selected scenarios. Teams should focus on it when explaining service registration in real systems, they compare it with order never matters, and they should avoid the trap of changing order blindly. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

{

    {
```

```csharp
        Console.WriteLine(orderSensitive);
    }
}
```

## 3. Service lifetimes

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Service lifetimes**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q3.1 What is singleton scoped transient basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Singleton scoped transient basics means service lifetime controls reuse, scope, and disposal behavior across the app. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with all services can share one lifetime, and they should avoid the trap of choosing lifetimes by habit. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<SingletonService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<ScopedService>();
        builder.Services.AddTransient<TransientService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class SingletonService { } class ScopedService { } class TransientService { }
    }
}
```

### Q3.2 How does singleton trade-offs in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Singleton trade-offs means singletons are reused broadly and must be thread-safe and dependency-safe for long application lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton everything for performance, and they should avoid the trap of putting request-specific assumptions into singletons. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool singletonNeedsThreadSafety = true;
        Console.WriteLine(singletonNeedsThreadSafety);
    }
}
```

### Q3.3 Why does scoped lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scoped lifetime behavior means scoped services typically live for a request or explicit scope and fit request-bound operations. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton assumptions for request data, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting scope boundaries. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}
```

### Q3.4 When should you use transient lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Transient lifetime behavior means transients create a new instance per resolution and fit stateless or lightweight services. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with reuse assumptions for transients, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring creation cost and disposal expectations. Example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo3_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool lightweight = true;
        Console.WriteLine(lightweight);
    }
}
```

### Q3.5 What problem does capturing scoped in singleton bug in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Capturing scoped in singleton bug means injecting shorter-lived services into longer-lived services can create invalid object graphs. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with lifetime mismatch is harmless, and they should avoid the trap of allowing scoped services to leak into singletons. ### Q3.19 Why does singleton scoped transient basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Singleton scoped transient basics means service lifetime controls reuse, scope, and disposal behavior across the app. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with all services can share one lifetime, and they should avoid the trap of choosing lifetimes by habit. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;
```

```csharp
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddSingleton<SingletonService>();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<ScopedService>();
        builder.Services.AddTransient<TransientService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class SingletonService { } class ScopedService { } class TransientService { }
    }
}
```

### Q3.20 When should you use singleton trade-offs in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Singleton trade-offs means singletons are reused broadly and must be thread-safe and dependency-safe for long application lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton everything for performance, and they should avoid the trap of putting request-specific assumptions into singletons. ### Q3.50 How does singleton trade-offs in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Singleton trade-offs means singletons are reused broadly and must be thread-safe and dependency-safe for long application lifetimes. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with singleton everything for performance, and they should avoid the trap of putting request-specific assumptions into singletons. ### Q3.52 When should you use transient lifetime behavior in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with reuse assumptions for transients, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring creation cost and disposal expectations. **Answer:** Singleton scoped transient basics means service lifetime controls reuse, scope, and disposal behavior across the app. Teams should focus on it when explaining service lifetimes in real systems, they compare it with all services can share one lifetime, and they should avoid the trap of choosing lifetimes by habit. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

```csharp
        builder.Services.AddTransient<TransientService>();

        class SingletonService { } class ScopedService { } class TransientService { }
```
```csharp
    }
}
```

## 4. Constructor injection

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Constructor injection**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q4.1 What problem does constructor injection basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor injection basics means constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and required at object creation time. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with hidden property injection everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of creating objects with missing required collaborators. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class OrdersController { public OrdersController(IOrderService service) { Service = service; } public IOrderService Service { get; } }
        interface IOrderService { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OrdersController).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q4.2 How would you explain required dependency clarity in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Required dependency clarity means constructor parameters show what a type truly needs to do its work. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with ambient dependencies only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding important collaborators behind globals or locators. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock, ILogger logger) { } }
        interface IClock { } interface ILogger { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceService).GetConstructors().Single().GetParameters().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q4.3 Why is constructor size and design smell in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor size and design smell means too many constructor dependencies can reveal that a type has too many responsibilities. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with big constructors are always fine, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cohesion signals. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class LargeService { public LargeService(IA a, IB b, IC c, ID d) { } }
        interface IA { } interface IB { } interface IC { } interface ID { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(LargeService).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q4.4 How can optional dependencies and alternatives in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Optional dependencies and alternatives means optional behavior often needs better design than a long list of nullable constructor arguments. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with nullable dependency sprawl, and they should avoid the trap of forcing constructor signatures to model every variation poorly. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool optionalDependency = false;
        Console.WriteLine(optionalDependency);
    }
}
```

### Q4.5 What is testability benefits of constructor injection in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Testability benefits of constructor injection means constructor injection makes substitution easier in unit tests and composition easier in startup. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with manual mutation after construction, and they should avoid the trap of making tests depend on hidden state changes. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var sut = new BillingService(new FakeGateway());
        Console.WriteLine(sut is not null);
        interface IGateway { } class FakeGateway : IGateway { } class BillingService { public BillingService(IGateway gateway) { } }
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with nullable dependency sprawl, and they should avoid the trap of forcing constructor signatures to model every variation poorly. ### Q4.13 What is constructor injection basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor injection basics means constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and required at object creation time. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with hidden property injection everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of creating objects with missing required collaborators. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_13
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class OrdersController { public OrdersController(IOrderService service) { Service = service; } public IOrderService Service { get; } }
        interface IOrderService { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OrdersController).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q4.14 How does required dependency clarity in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Required dependency clarity means constructor parameters show what a type truly needs to do its work. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with ambient dependencies only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding important collaborators behind globals or locators. Example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_14
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock, ILogger logger) { } }
        interface IClock { } interface ILogger { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceService).GetConstructors().Single().GetParameters().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q4.15 Why does constructor size and design smell in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor size and design smell means too many constructor dependencies can reveal that a type has too many responsibilities. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with big constructors are always fine, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cohesion signals. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_15
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class LargeService { public LargeService(IA a, IB b, IC c, ID d) { } }
        interface IA { } interface IB { } interface IC { } interface ID { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(LargeService).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with manual mutation after construction, and they should avoid the trap of making tests depend on hidden state changes. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{

    {

    }
}
```

### Q4.43 Why is constructor injection basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor injection basics means constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and required at object creation time. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with hidden property injection everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of creating objects with missing required collaborators. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_43
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class OrdersController { public OrdersController(IOrderService service) { Service = service; } public IOrderService Service { get; } }
        interface IOrderService { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OrdersController).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q4.44 How can required dependency clarity in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Required dependency clarity means constructor parameters show what a type truly needs to do its work. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with ambient dependencies only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding important collaborators behind globals or locators. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_44
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class InvoiceService { public InvoiceService(IClock clock, ILogger logger) { } }
        interface IClock { } interface ILogger { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceService).GetConstructors().Single().GetParameters().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q4.45 What is constructor size and design smell in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor size and design smell means too many constructor dependencies can reveal that a type has too many responsibilities. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with big constructors are always fine, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cohesion signals. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo4_45
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class LargeService { public LargeService(IA a, IB b, IC c, ID d) { } }
        interface IA { } interface IB { } interface IC { } interface ID { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(LargeService).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q4.46 How does optional dependencies and alternatives in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Optional dependencies and alternatives means optional behavior often needs better design than a long list of nullable constructor arguments. Teams should focus on it when explaining constructor injection in real systems, they compare it with nullable dependency sprawl, and they should avoid the trap of forcing constructor signatures to model every variation poorly. ### Q4.74 How would you explain required dependency clarity in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

## 5. Interface-based design

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Interface-based design**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q5.1 What is interfaces as DI contracts in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Interfaces as di contracts means interfaces often represent service contracts so implementations can vary cleanly behind stable abstractions. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with concrete-type coupling everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of adding interfaces with no substitution value. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        Console.WriteLine(new SystemClock().UtcNow.Year > 2000);
    }
}
```

### Q5.2 How does abstraction for replaceability in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Abstraction for replaceability means interface-based design supports swapping implementations for tests, environment differences, or feature changes. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with one concrete implementation forever, and they should avoid the trap of hard-wiring callers to implementation details. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IReportStore { void Save(); }
        class SqlReportStore : IReportStore { public void Save() => Console.WriteLine("saved"); }
        new SqlReportStore().Save();
    }
}
```

### Q5.3 Why does small focused contracts in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Small focused contracts means interfaces work best when they express cohesive capabilities instead of giant unrelated surfaces. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with fat interfaces, and they should avoid the trap of forcing consumers to depend on irrelevant members. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

### Q5.7 Why is interfaces as DI contracts in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Interfaces as di contracts means interfaces often represent service contracts so implementations can vary cleanly behind stable abstractions. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with concrete-type coupling everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of adding interfaces with no substitution value. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_7
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        Console.WriteLine(new SystemClock().UtcNow.Year > 2000);
    }
}
```

### Q5.8 How can abstraction for replaceability in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Abstraction for replaceability means interface-based design supports swapping implementations for tests, environment differences, or feature changes. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with one concrete implementation forever, and they should avoid the trap of hard-wiring callers to implementation details. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_8
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IReportStore { void Save(); }
        class SqlReportStore : IReportStore { public void Save() => Console.WriteLine("saved"); }
        new SqlReportStore().Save();
    }
}
```

### Q5.37 What problem does interfaces as DI contracts in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Interfaces as di contracts means interfaces often represent service contracts so implementations can vary cleanly behind stable abstractions. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with concrete-type coupling everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of adding interfaces with no substitution value. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_37
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        Console.WriteLine(new SystemClock().UtcNow.Year > 2000);
    }
}
```

### Q5.38 How would you explain abstraction for replaceability in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Abstraction for replaceability means interface-based design supports swapping implementations for tests, environment differences, or feature changes. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with one concrete implementation forever, and they should avoid the trap of hard-wiring callers to implementation details. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_38
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IReportStore { void Save(); }
        class SqlReportStore : IReportStore { public void Save() => Console.WriteLine("saved"); }
        new SqlReportStore().Save();
    }
}
```

### Q5.39 Why is small focused contracts in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Small focused contracts means interfaces work best when they express cohesive capabilities instead of giant unrelated surfaces. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with fat interfaces, and they should avoid the trap of forcing consumers to depend on irrelevant members. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;

{
    public static void Run()
    {

        Console.WriteLine(typeof(INotifier).Name);
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with interface evangelism only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping practical design trade-offs. ### Q5.67 Why does interfaces as DI contracts in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Interfaces as di contracts means interfaces often represent service contracts so implementations can vary cleanly behind stable abstractions. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with concrete-type coupling everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of adding interfaces with no substitution value. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_67
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        Console.WriteLine(new SystemClock().UtcNow.Year > 2000);
    }
}
```

### Q5.68 When should you use abstraction for replaceability in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Abstraction for replaceability means interface-based design supports swapping implementations for tests, environment differences, or feature changes. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with one concrete implementation forever, and they should avoid the trap of hard-wiring callers to implementation details. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_68
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IReportStore { void Save(); }
        class SqlReportStore : IReportStore { public void Save() => Console.WriteLine("saved"); }
        new SqlReportStore().Save();
    }
}
```

### Q5.69 What problem does small focused contracts in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Small focused contracts means interfaces work best when they express cohesive capabilities instead of giant unrelated surfaces. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with fat interfaces, and they should avoid the trap of forcing consumers to depend on irrelevant members. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{

    {

        Console.WriteLine(typeof(INotifier).Name);
    }
}
```

**Answer:** Di and boundary design means interfaces often sit at architectural boundaries where policies should not depend on low-level details. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with abstractions everywhere blindly, and they should avoid the trap of abstracting classes that do not benefit from it. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_97
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IClock { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }
        class SystemClock : IClock { public DateTime UtcNow => DateTime.UtcNow; }
        Console.WriteLine(new SystemClock().UtcNow.Year > 2000);
    }
}
```

### Q5.98 How does abstraction for replaceability in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Abstraction for replaceability means interface-based design supports swapping implementations for tests, environment differences, or feature changes. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with one concrete implementation forever, and they should avoid the trap of hard-wiring callers to implementation details. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_98
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface IReportStore { void Save(); }
        class SqlReportStore : IReportStore { public void Save() => Console.WriteLine("saved"); }
        new SqlReportStore().Save();
    }
}
```

### Q5.99 Why does small focused contracts in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Small focused contracts means interfaces work best when they express cohesive capabilities instead of giant unrelated surfaces. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with fat interfaces, and they should avoid the trap of forcing consumers to depend on irrelevant members. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_99
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        interface INotifier { void Send(); }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(INotifier).Name);
    }
}
```

### Q5.100 When should you use DI and boundary design in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Di and boundary design means interfaces often sit at architectural boundaries where policies should not depend on low-level details. Teams should focus on it when explaining interface-based design in real systems, they compare it with abstractions everywhere blindly, and they should avoid the trap of abstracting classes that do not benefit from it. Example: during a service-registration review, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo5_100
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool abstractionNeeded = true;
        Console.WriteLine(abstractionNeeded);
    }
}
```

## 6. Options pattern

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Options pattern**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q6.1 What problem does options pattern basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Options pattern basics means the options pattern binds configuration into typed classes and injects them as dependencies. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with raw string config access everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of scattering key names through service code. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo6_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.Configure<MyOptions>(builder.Configuration.GetSection("MyApp"));
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class MyOptions { public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
    }
}
```

### Q6.2 How would you explain options with DI integration in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Options with di integration means options participate in DI so services can consume structured settings cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with manual config object wiring, and they should avoid the trap of treating config as a special case outside composition. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo6_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddOptions<MyOptions>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class MyOptions { }
    }
}
```

### Q6.3 Why is IOptions IOptionsSnapshot and IOptionsMonitor in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Ioptions ioptionssnapshot and ioptionsmonitor means different options interfaces match different lifetime and reload needs. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with one options type for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using the wrong options abstraction for runtime behavior. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo6_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class ComplexOptions { public InnerOptions Inner { get; set; } = new(); }
        class InnerOptions { public string Value { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(ComplexOptions).Name);
    }
}
```

### Q6.4 How can options class design in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Options class design means option classes should stay cohesive and represent one settings concern well. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with mega option bags, and they should avoid the trap of binding unrelated settings into one type. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo6_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.Configure<MyOptions>(_ => { });
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class MyOptions { }
    }
}
```

**Answer:** Options validation importance means typed config is stronger when paired with validation and fail-fast rules. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with binding alone is enough, and they should avoid the trap of assuming shape equals correctness. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

}
```

### Q6.29 What is options validation importance in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Options validation importance means typed config is stronger when paired with validation and fail-fast rules. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with binding alone is enough, and they should avoid the trap of assuming shape equals correctness. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.
