### Q6.31 Why does options pattern basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Options pattern basics means the options pattern binds configuration into typed classes and injects them as dependencies. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with raw string config access everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of scattering key names through service code. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo6_31
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.Configure<MyOptions>(builder.Configuration.GetSection("MyApp"));
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class MyOptions { public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
    }
}
```

### Q6.32 When should you use options with DI integration in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Options with di integration means options participate in DI so services can consume structured settings cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with manual config object wiring, and they should avoid the trap of treating config as a special case outside composition. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo6_32
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddOptions<MyOptions>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class MyOptions { }
    }
}
```

### Q6.33 What problem does IOptions IOptionsSnapshot and IOptionsMonitor in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Ioptions ioptionssnapshot and ioptionsmonitor means different options interfaces match different lifetime and reload needs. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with one options type for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using the wrong options abstraction for runtime behavior. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo6_33
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class ComplexOptions { public InnerOptions Inner { get; set; } = new(); }
        class InnerOptions { public string Value { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(ComplexOptions).Name);
    }
}
```

### Q6.34 How would you explain options class design in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Options class design means option classes should stay cohesive and represent one settings concern well. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with mega option bags, and they should avoid the trap of binding unrelated settings into one type. ### Q6.93 What is IOptions IOptionsSnapshot and IOptionsMonitor in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Ioptions ioptionssnapshot and ioptionsmonitor means different options interfaces match different lifetime and reload needs. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with one options type for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using the wrong options abstraction for runtime behavior. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

```csharp
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
```

```csharp
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class ComplexOptions { public InnerOptions Inner { get; set; } = new(); }
        class InnerOptions { public string Value { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(ComplexOptions).Name);
    }
}
```

### Q6.94 How does options class design in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Options class design means option classes should stay cohesive and represent one settings concern well. Teams should focus on it when explaining options pattern in real systems, they compare it with mega option bags, and they should avoid the trap of binding unrelated settings into one type. ### Q6.97 What problem does options pattern basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

```csharp
}
```

## 7. Scoped services in web requests

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Scoped services in web requests**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q7.1 What is request scope basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Request scope basics means scoped services usually live for one web request and are disposed when that scope ends. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with application-wide singleton assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting request scope boundaries. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<RequestContext>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class RequestContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.2 How does request specific state handling in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Request specific state handling means scoped services fit per-request context such as user or unit-of-work style state. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with singleton request state, and they should avoid the trap of sharing request data across users accidentally. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}
```

### Q7.3 Why does scope creation in background work in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scope creation in background work means background tasks may need explicit scopes when they use scoped services outside HTTP requests. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with ambient scope assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of resolving scoped services with no valid scope. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var scope = new ServiceCollection().AddScoped<RequestContext>().BuildServiceProvider().CreateScope();
        Console.WriteLine(scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<RequestContext>() is not null);
        class RequestContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.4 When should you use dbcontext and scoped lifetime fit in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Dbcontext and scoped lifetime fit means request-bound data access components are commonly scoped to align with request work units. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with singleton data context designs, and they should avoid the trap of reusing request-bound resources too broadly. Example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<AppDbContext>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class AppDbContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.5 What problem does disposal at scope end in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Disposal at scope end means scoped service disposal happens with the scope and affects owned dependencies and resources. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with manual disposal confusion, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring scope-owned resource cleanup. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a controller refactor, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{

    {

        Console.WriteLine(disposedWithScope);
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with manual disposal confusion, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring scope-owned resource cleanup. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_23
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool disposedWithScope = true;
        Console.WriteLine(disposedWithScope);
    }
}
```

### Q7.24 How can scoped service interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scoped service interview framing means strong answers tie scoped lifetimes to HTTP request boundaries and explicit scope creation. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with request lifetime slogans only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping non-request scenarios. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

```csharp
using System;

    {
        Console.WriteLine("Scoped services fit request-oriented work and explicit scopes");
    }
}
```

### Q7.25 What is request scope basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Request scope basics means scoped services usually live for one web request and are disposed when that scope ends. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with application-wide singleton assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting request scope boundaries. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_25
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<RequestContext>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class RequestContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.26 How does request specific state handling in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Request specific state handling means scoped services fit per-request context such as user or unit-of-work style state. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with singleton request state, and they should avoid the trap of sharing request data across users accidentally. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_26
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}
```

### Q7.27 Why does scope creation in background work in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scope creation in background work means background tasks may need explicit scopes when they use scoped services outside HTTP requests. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with ambient scope assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of resolving scoped services with no valid scope. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_27
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var scope = new ServiceCollection().AddScoped<RequestContext>().BuildServiceProvider().CreateScope();
        Console.WriteLine(scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<RequestContext>() is not null);
        class RequestContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.28 When should you use dbcontext and scoped lifetime fit in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Dbcontext and scoped lifetime fit means request-bound data access components are commonly scoped to align with request work units. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with singleton data context designs, and they should avoid the trap of reusing request-bound resources too broadly. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_28
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<AppDbContext>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class AppDbContext { }
    }
}
```

```csharp
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;
```

```csharp
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Scoped services fit request-oriented work and explicit scopes");
    }
}
```

### Q7.55 Why is request scope basics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Request scope basics means scoped services usually live for one web request and are disposed when that scope ends. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with application-wide singleton assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting request scope boundaries. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_55
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<RequestContext>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class RequestContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.56 How can request specific state handling in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Request specific state handling means scoped services fit per-request context such as user or unit-of-work style state. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with singleton request state, and they should avoid the trap of sharing request data across users accidentally. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_56
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}
```

### Q7.57 What is scope creation in background work in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scope creation in background work means background tasks may need explicit scopes when they use scoped services outside HTTP requests. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with ambient scope assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of resolving scoped services with no valid scope. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_57
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var scope = new ServiceCollection().AddScoped<RequestContext>().BuildServiceProvider().CreateScope();
        Console.WriteLine(scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<RequestContext>() is not null);
        class RequestContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.58 How does dbcontext and scoped lifetime fit in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Dbcontext and scoped lifetime fit means request-bound data access components are commonly scoped to align with request work units. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with singleton data context designs, and they should avoid the trap of reusing request-bound resources too broadly. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_58
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<AppDbContext>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class AppDbContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.59 Why does disposal at scope end in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Disposal at scope end means scoped service disposal happens with the scope and affects owned dependencies and resources. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with manual disposal confusion, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring scope-owned resource cleanup. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_59
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool disposedWithScope = true;
        Console.WriteLine(disposedWithScope);
    }
}
```

Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with singleton request state, and they should avoid the trap of sharing request data across users accidentally. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_86
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool requestBound = true;
        Console.WriteLine(requestBound);
    }
}
```

### Q7.87 Why is scope creation in background work in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scope creation in background work means background tasks may need explicit scopes when they use scoped services outside HTTP requests. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with ambient scope assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of resolving scoped services with no valid scope. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_87
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var scope = new ServiceCollection().AddScoped<RequestContext>().BuildServiceProvider().CreateScope();
        Console.WriteLine(scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<RequestContext>() is not null);
        class RequestContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.88 How can dbcontext and scoped lifetime fit in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Dbcontext and scoped lifetime fit means request-bound data access components are commonly scoped to align with request work units. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with singleton data context designs, and they should avoid the trap of reusing request-bound resources too broadly. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_88
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<AppDbContext>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        class AppDbContext { }
    }
}
```

### Q7.89 What is disposal at scope end in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Disposal at scope end means scoped service disposal happens with the scope and affects owned dependencies and resources. Teams should focus on it when explaining scoped services in web requests in real systems, they compare it with manual disposal confusion, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring scope-owned resource cleanup. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo7_89
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool disposedWithScope = true;
        Console.WriteLine(disposedWithScope);
    }
}
```

## 8. Testing and mocking

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Testing and mocking**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q8.1 What problem does DI and test seam creation in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Di and test seam creation means dependency injection makes unit testing easier by allowing collaborators to be substituted cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with hard-wired dependencies in services, and they should avoid the trap of designing classes with no replacement seam. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo8_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new BillingService(new FakeGateway());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IGateway { } class FakeGateway : IGateway { } class BillingService { public BillingService(IGateway gateway) { } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.2 How would you explain fakes and stubs with injected interfaces in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Fakes and stubs with injected interfaces means tests can inject fakes stubs or mocks through constructor seams created by DI-friendly design. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with real infrastructure in unit tests, and they should avoid the trap of forcing network or database calls into small tests. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo8_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var fake = new FakeClock();
        Console.WriteLine(fake.UtcNow.Year > 2000);
        class FakeClock { public DateTime UtcNow => new DateTime(2025, 1, 1); }
    }
}
```

### Q8.3 Why is composition for test setup in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Composition for test setup means services should be easy to construct in tests without booting the entire host when possible. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with full host startup for every unit test, and they should avoid the trap of making tests heavier than needed. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo8_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var direct = new MailService(new StubSender());
        Console.WriteLine(direct is not null);
        interface ISender { } class StubSender : ISender { } class MailService { public MailService(ISender sender) { } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.4 How can container usage in tests in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container usage in tests means some tests use the real DI container intentionally, but many unit tests can stay simpler with direct construction. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with container everywhere in tests, and they should avoid the trap of using the host when direct wiring is clearer. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo8_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool useContainerInEveryUnitTest = false;
        Console.WriteLine(useContainerInEveryUnitTest);
    }
}
```

### Q8.5 What is mocking boundaries and overuse in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Mocking boundaries and overuse means DI helps mocking, but too much mocking can couple tests to implementation details. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with mock every collaborator always, and they should avoid the trap of verifying internal choreography instead of behavior. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo8_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool overMocked = false;
        Console.WriteLine(overMocked);
    }
}
```

### Q8.6 How does testing interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Testing interview framing means good answers connect DI seams to test readability and confidence layers. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with mocking tool syntax only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping design-for-testability reasoning. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower.

```csharp
using System.Linq;

using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("DI should make seams testable, not force heavy test setup");
    }
}
```

```csharp
        bool useContainerInEveryUnitTest = false;
        Console.WriteLine(useContainerInEveryUnitTest);
    }
}
```

### Q8.17 What problem does mocking boundaries and overuse in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Mocking boundaries and overuse means DI helps mocking, but too much mocking can couple tests to implementation details. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with mock every collaborator always, and they should avoid the trap of verifying internal choreography instead of behavior. Example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a web request scope investigation, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo8_17
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool overMocked = false;
        Console.WriteLine(overMocked);
    }
}
```

### Q8.19 Why is DI and test seam creation in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Di and test seam creation means dependency injection makes unit testing easier by allowing collaborators to be substituted cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with hard-wired dependencies in services, and they should avoid the trap of designing classes with no replacement seam. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{
    public static void Run()

        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IGateway { } class FakeGateway : IGateway { } class BillingService { public BillingService(IGateway gateway) { } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.20 How can fakes and stubs with injected interfaces in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Fakes and stubs with injected interfaces means tests can inject fakes stubs or mocks through constructor seams created by DI-friendly design. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with real infrastructure in unit tests, and they should avoid the trap of forcing network or database calls into small tests. Example: during a service-registration review, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo8_20
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var fake = new FakeClock();
        Console.WriteLine(fake.UtcNow.Year > 2000);
        class FakeClock { public DateTime UtcNow => new DateTime(2025, 1, 1); }
    }
}
```

### Q8.21 What is composition for test setup in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Composition for test setup means services should be easy to construct in tests without booting the entire host when possible. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with full host startup for every unit test, and they should avoid the trap of making tests heavier than needed. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo8_21
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var direct = new MailService(new StubSender());
        Console.WriteLine(direct is not null);
        interface ISender { } class StubSender : ISender { } class MailService { public MailService(ISender sender) { } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.22 How does container usage in tests in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container usage in tests means some tests use the real DI container intentionally, but many unit tests can stay simpler with direct construction. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with container everywhere in tests, and they should avoid the trap of using the host when direct wiring is clearer. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;

{
    public static void Run()
    {

        Console.WriteLine(useContainerInEveryUnitTest);
```
```csharp
    }
}
```

### Q8.50 How would you explain fakes and stubs with injected interfaces in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Fakes and stubs with injected interfaces means tests can inject fakes stubs or mocks through constructor seams created by DI-friendly design. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with real infrastructure in unit tests, and they should avoid the trap of forcing network or database calls into small tests. ```csharp

```csharp
    }
```

### Q8.51 Why is composition for test setup in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Composition for test setup means services should be easy to construct in tests without booting the entire host when possible. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with full host startup for every unit test, and they should avoid the trap of making tests heavier than needed. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

```csharp
    }
}
```

### Q8.52 How can container usage in tests in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container usage in tests means some tests use the real DI container intentionally, but many unit tests can stay simpler with direct construction. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with container everywhere in tests, and they should avoid the trap of using the host when direct wiring is clearer. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

    public static void Run()

        Console.WriteLine(useContainerInEveryUnitTest);
    }
}
```

### Q8.53 What is mocking boundaries and overuse in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Mocking boundaries and overuse means DI helps mocking, but too much mocking can couple tests to implementation details. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with mock every collaborator always, and they should avoid the trap of verifying internal choreography instead of behavior. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

```csharp
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{

        Console.WriteLine(overMocked);
    }
}
```

### Q8.79 Why does DI and test seam creation in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Di and test seam creation means dependency injection makes unit testing easier by allowing collaborators to be substituted cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with hard-wired dependencies in services, and they should avoid the trap of designing classes with no replacement seam. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new BillingService(new FakeGateway());
        Console.WriteLine(service is not null);
        interface IGateway { } class FakeGateway : IGateway { } class BillingService { public BillingService(IGateway gateway) { } }
```
```csharp
    }
}
```

### Q8.81 What problem does composition for test setup in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with container everywhere in tests, and they should avoid the trap of using the host when direct wiring is clearer. ### Q8.83 Why is mocking boundaries and overuse in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Mocking boundaries and overuse means DI helps mocking, but too much mocking can couple tests to implementation details. Teams should focus on it when explaining testing and mocking in real systems, they compare it with mock every collaborator always, and they should avoid the trap of verifying internal choreography instead of behavior. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

        Console.WriteLine(overMocked);
```
```csharp
    }
}
```

## 9. Anti-patterns

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Anti-patterns**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q9.1 What is service locator misuse in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Service locator misuse means using the provider as a general service locator hides dependencies and weakens design clarity. Teams should focus on it when explaining anti-patterns in real systems, they compare it with constructor injection discipline, and they should avoid the trap of pulling dependencies from IServiceProvider everywhere. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a service-registration review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo9_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool serviceLocator = false;
        Console.WriteLine(serviceLocator);
    }
}
```

### Q9.2 How does god services and over-injection in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** God services and over-injection means too many dependencies in one service often reveal broken responsibilities. Teams should focus on it when explaining anti-patterns in real systems, they compare it with wide constructors are harmless, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring design smell signals. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo9_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int dependencyCount = 7;
        Console.WriteLine(dependencyCount);
    }
}
```

### Q9.3 Why does lifetime mismatch anti-patterns in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Lifetime mismatch anti-patterns means mixing lifetimes incorrectly can create bugs leaks and invalid runtime behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining anti-patterns in real systems, they compare it with lifetime mismatch is safe enough, and they should avoid the trap of injecting scoped services into singletons. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo9_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool mismatch = true;
        Console.WriteLine(mismatch);
    }
}
```

### Q9.4 When should you use registration sprawl in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Registration sprawl means unstructured registration code can become hard to reason about as the app grows. Teams should focus on it when explaining anti-patterns in real systems, they compare it with one giant startup block forever, and they should avoid the trap of letting composition become opaque. Example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

{
    public static void Run()
    {
        string startupBlock = "too-large";
        Console.WriteLine(startupBlock);
    }
}
```

### Q9.5 What problem does container overreach in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Container overreach means not every problem should be solved with more DI indirection or framework wiring. Teams should focus on it when explaining anti-patterns in real systems, they compare it with DI as universal answer, and they should avoid the trap of abstracting and registering every tiny class by default. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a controller refactor, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

## 10. Troubleshooting resolution issues

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Troubleshooting resolution issues**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q10.1 What problem does missing registration diagnosis in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Missing registration diagnosis means resolution failures often start with a missing or incorrect registration in the composition root. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with container is broken thinking, and they should avoid the trap of debugging without checking registration state first. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { } class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}
```

### Q10.2 How would you explain lifetime exception analysis in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Lifetime exception analysis means runtime DI errors often point to lifetime mismatches or invalid scope usage. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with registration exists therefore it must be correct, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring lifetime in failure analysis. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

    public static void Run()
    {

        Console.WriteLine(lifetimeProblem);
    }
}
```

### Q10.3 Why is constructor graph debugging in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor graph debugging means resolution failures may come from deeper constructor dependencies not just the top-level requested type. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with only the requested service matters, and they should avoid the trap of stopping at the first visible type name. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the composition boundary stays clearer. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class ReportHandler { public ReportHandler(IOrderService service) { } }
        interface IOrderService { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(ReportHandler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q10.4 How can scope and disposal troubleshooting in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scope and disposal troubleshooting means some bugs appear because a scoped service is accessed after disposal or outside a valid scope. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with scope always exists assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring disposal timing. Example: during a controller refactor, so the dependency graph becomes easier to explain. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool outsideScope = false;
        Console.WriteLine(outsideScope);
    }
}
```

### Q10.5 What is startup validation and diagnostics in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Startup validation and diagnostics means logging and startup validation can surface DI problems earlier and make failures more actionable. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with runtime surprise discovery only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping validation opportunities. ### Q10.13 What is missing registration diagnosis in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Missing registration diagnosis means resolution failures often start with a missing or incorrect registration in the composition root. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with container is broken thinking, and they should avoid the trap of debugging without checking registration state first. Example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an options-binding cleanup, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_13
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { } class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}
```

### Q10.14 How does lifetime exception analysis in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Lifetime exception analysis means runtime DI errors often point to lifetime mismatches or invalid scope usage. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with registration exists therefore it must be correct, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring lifetime in failure analysis. Example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while hardening composition-root conventions, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_14
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool lifetimeProblem = true;
        Console.WriteLine(lifetimeProblem);
    }
}
```

### Q10.15 Why does constructor graph debugging in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor graph debugging means resolution failures may come from deeper constructor dependencies not just the top-level requested type. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with only the requested service matters, and they should avoid the trap of stopping at the first visible type name. Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_15
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class ReportHandler { public ReportHandler(IOrderService service) { } }
        interface IOrderService { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(ReportHandler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q10.16 When should you use scope and disposal troubleshooting in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scope and disposal troubleshooting means some bugs appear because a scoped service is accessed after disposal or outside a valid scope. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with scope always exists assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring disposal timing. Example: during a web request scope investigation, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

Example: while reviewing test seams in an API layer, so the registration model becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a controller refactor, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

### Q10.46 How does scope and disposal troubleshooting in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scope and disposal troubleshooting means some bugs appear because a scoped service is accessed after disposal or outside a valid scope. ### Q10.78 How does troubleshooting interview framing in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Troubleshooting interview framing means good answers describe a step-by-step graph and lifetime debugging workflow. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with guess-first advice, and they should avoid the trap of skipping systematic diagnosis. Example: during a DI container startup failure, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while debugging a scoped-service exception, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_78
{

}
```

### Q10.79 Why does missing registration diagnosis in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Missing registration diagnosis means resolution failures often start with a missing or incorrect registration in the composition root. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with container is broken thinking, and they should avoid the trap of debugging without checking registration state first. Example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: during a DI container startup failure, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_79
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder();
        builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>();
        Console.WriteLine(builder.Services.Count > 0);
        interface IOrderService { } class OrderService : IOrderService { }
    }
}
```

### Q10.80 When should you use lifetime exception analysis in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Lifetime exception analysis means runtime DI errors often point to lifetime mismatches or invalid scope usage. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with registration exists therefore it must be correct, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring lifetime in failure analysis. Example: during a service-registration review, so the test seam becomes easier to reason about. Another example: while diagnosing a service locator smell, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_80
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool lifetimeProblem = true;
        Console.WriteLine(lifetimeProblem);
    }
}
```

### Q10.81 What problem does constructor graph debugging in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Constructor graph debugging means resolution failures may come from deeper constructor dependencies not just the top-level requested type. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with only the requested service matters, and they should avoid the trap of stopping at the first visible type name. Example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the lifetime rule becomes easier to defend. Another example: during a service-registration review, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_81
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        class ReportHandler { public ReportHandler(IOrderService service) { } }
        interface IOrderService { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(ReportHandler).GetConstructors().Length);
    }
}
```

### Q10.82 How would you explain scope and disposal troubleshooting in ASP.NET Core dependency management?

**Answer:** Scope and disposal troubleshooting means some bugs appear because a scoped service is accessed after disposal or outside a valid scope. Teams should focus on it when explaining troubleshooting resolution issues in real systems, they compare it with scope always exists assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring disposal timing. Example: during an options-binding cleanup, so startup behavior becomes easier to validate. Another example: while tracing a lifetime mismatch, so the composition boundary stays clearer.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection;

public static class Demo10_82
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        bool outsideScope = false;
        Console.WriteLine(outsideScope);
    }
}
```
